aiSummary.explanation: This track could not be positively identified in any copyright database, with no PRO registrations, no confirmed master rights holder, no known writers or publishers, and no label information available. The extremely low confidence score (15%) combined with a critical risk level indicates the system could not reliably match this audio to any known copyrighted work. However, the absence of copyright data does NOT mean the track is free to use—it may simply be unregistered, under a different title, or belong to an independent artist. All audio recordings are automatically protected by copyright upon creation in most jurisdictions, regardless of registration status.
actionableSteps: Use music identification apps like Shazam or SoundHound to attempt to identify the track and its rights holders independently.
If the track cannot be identified, consider using royalty-free or Creative Commons music from established libraries like Epidemic Sound, Artlist, or YouTube Audio Library instead.
Consult with a music licensing professional or entertainment attorney before using this track in any commercial or public project.

aiSummary.labelInfo: Radical Dreamland appears to be an independent label/publisher associated with composer Lena Raine, a prominent video game composer known for work on Celeste and Minecraft; no specific licensing portal or creator-friendly policies were found in search results.
aiSummary.licensingVerdict: A license is required to use this track. Lena Raine is an established composer, and her works are protected under copyright law with master and publishing rights held by Radical Dreamland.
aiSummary.explanation: This track is an original composition by Lena Raine, a well-known video game composer, with both master rights and publishing rights held by Radical Dreamland. No PRO registrations were found, which is common for video game soundtracks that may be administered outside traditional PRO systems. The 58% confidence and lack of clear licensing information in search results suggests this may be a niche catalog, but the absence of a public licensing portal does NOT mean the work is free to use. Both sync (for composition) and master use licenses would be required for any commercial or derivative use.
actionableSteps: Contact Radical Dreamland or Lena Raine directly through official channels (Bandcamp, website, or social media) to inquire about sync licensing options.
If this track is part of a video game soundtrack, verify whether the game publisher (e.g., Matt Makes Games for Celeste, Microsoft/Mojang for Minecraft) holds additional rights that need clearance.
Consider using a music clearance service or entertainment attorney to negotiate a license if you plan to use this in a commercial project.

webSearchSources.title: Sync License Agreement Question (Between Artist and Producer)
webSearchSources.snippet: # Sync License Agreement Question (Between Artist and Producer). A question on the clearance side of getting permissions of sync from a producer…. Example: An artist buys a beat from producer, the beat license says sync can only be used for a music video and nothing more. Artist decides they want to reach out to music supervisors and get a sync on tv, while being a one stop. Artist talks to producer, producer verbally says the song is totally cool to use for sync, but the original agreement says otherwise. I am assuming music supervisors want to see this in legal writing (correct me if I’m wrong). Does anyone have experience with this scenario? Would the artist just need an updated agreement for sync from producer? If so whether you’re an artist, producer, or involved in anyway I’d very love to chat with you on your experiences and what the agreement looks like. Reddit Rules    Privacy Policy    User Agreement    Accessibility   Reddit, Inc.

licensingPath: This is a cover of 'At Doom's Gate' originally composed by Bobby Prince for the DOOM (1993) video game soundtrack. The original composition is owned by id Software (now Microsoft/Zenimax). To legally use this Daniel Tidwell cover recording, you would need: (1) Sync license from Microsoft/id Software for the underlying composition, (2) Permission or license from Daniel Tidwell for his specific recording/arrangement. For commercial use, contact Microsoft's licensing department and Daniel Tidwell directly. Note: Many video game soundtrack covers on YouTube operate in a gray area - formal commercial use requires proper clearance from both parties.
